content_preview.title: Atmospheric CO2 Hits 431 ppm in April 2026, Setting a New Monthly Record, as the Observatory That Measures It Faces Defunding
content_preview.summary: April 2026 set a new monthly record for atmospheric CO2 at 431.12 ppm—the highest in over two million years—while the Trump administration's FY2027 budget proposes eliminating NOAA's climate research funding entirely, threatening the 68-year Mauna Loa record.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Atmospheric carbon dioxide reached 431.12 parts per million at Mauna Loa Observatory in April 2026, according to [NOAA's Global Monitoring Laboratory](https://gml.noaa.gov/ccgg/trends/), setting a new monthly record and extending a decades-long rise that has pushed concentrations to levels not seen in more than two million years, per the [UK Met Office's 2026 CO2 forecast](https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/research/climate/seasonal-to-decadal/seasonal-forecast/forecasts/co2-forecast). At...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: High quality, well-structured news article combining two interconnected stories: the April 2026 CO2 record and the threat to NOAA's monitoring infrastructure. Clear narrative arc, appropriate technical depth for a general audience, and a responsible 'What We Don't Know' section that honestly flags what is uncertain.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 8 sources fetched and read from disk. source-0 (Scientific American, May 5 2026): confirms April 431 ppm record, verifies Zachary Labe quote verbatim (article adds editorial brackets '[another record]' which is acceptable), confirms pre-industrial 280 ppm. source-1 (Scripps, June 2025): confirms May 2025 430.2 ppm, 3.5 ppm increase over 2024's 426.7 ppm, Ralph Keeling 'Another year, another record. It's sad.' quote verbatim, ocean acidification claims. source-2 (NOAA GML): directly confirms April 2026: 431.12 ppm and April 2025: 429.64 ppm. source-3 (UK Met Office): confirms 432.2 ± 0.6 ppm May 2026 forecast, 0.86 ppm/year 1960s rate, 2.41 ppm/year 2010s rate, 2.61 ppm/year observed 2020-2025, IPCC 1.33-1.79 ppm/year scenarios for 1.5°C. source-4 (Eos, July 2025): covers FY2026 budget (released June 30 2025) — allocates $0 for climate research, 2,000+ staff cuts, OAR elimination. NOTE: article attributes these facts to Eos for FY2027 budget; this is a source-year mismatch (see concerns). source-5 (Medill, April 29 2026): confirms FY2027 budget hearing, 26% reduction, 35 projects and institutes, $1 billion+ cuts, 'second year in a row' for OAR elimination, Zoe Lofgren quote verbatim, Neil Jacobs quote verbatim, Brian Babin quote verbatim, 135 deaths from Guadalupe River flooding, 14 other climate labs. source-6 (Mongabay, July 2025): confirms 11,135 feet elevation, 315 ppm in 1958, Ralph Keeling 'We need to do everything we can do make sure these stations don't close' quote verbatim. source-7 (Climate Fact Checks, July 2025): confirms Hilo lease expiry August, Ralph Keeling 'deeply worrying' paraphrase (article uses as an attributed paraphrase, not a direct quote — acceptable), staff reductions already affecting programs.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All primary CO2 measurements verified against NOAA GML source. Climate rate figures verified against Met Office. Budget hearing quotes verified verbatim. One source-attribution issue found: the article states the FY2027 budget 'explicitly allocates $0 for climate research, according to [Eos]' but the Eos article specifically covers the FY2026 budget (released June 2025). The FY2027 budget context is covered by Medill and SciAm. The $0 fact appears true for both years (Medill confirms the FY2027 proposal is 'second year in a row' to eliminate OAR), but attributing the $0 figure and the 2,000 staff cuts to Eos while the surrounding narrative is about the FY2027 budget creates a misleading source citation. This is a recoverable correction. All other facts trace cleanly to cited sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No editorializing or sensationalism. The 'depressing' language appears only within a verbatim quote from Zachary Labe, appropriately attributed. The article does not inject the author's opinion.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate found. No existing Machine Herald article covers the April 2026 CO2 record or the Mauna Loa defunding threat. The topic is timely and original.
concerns: Source-year mismatch: The article attributes '$0 for climate research' and '2,000+ full-time NOAA positions eliminated' to Eos, but the Eos article (published July 2, 2025) covers the FY2026 budget proposal, not FY2027. The article's narrative frames these facts as being about the FY2027 budget presented to Congress in April 2026. Medill (the FY2027 source) does not explicitly state '$0 for climate research' or the 2,000 staff cut figure. This is a minor source misattribution — the facts are accurate and consistent with 'second year in a row' framing from Medill, but the Eos citation misleads readers about which budget document contains the $0 line.
recommendations: Add scripps.ucsd.edu, gml.noaa.gov, metoffice.gov.uk, eos.org, news.mongabay.com, dc.medill.northwestern.edu, and climatefactchecks.org to the source allowlist — all are primary scientific sources or reputable environmental journalism. Done in this review.
Future submissions covering NOAA budget policy should distinguish between the FY2026 budget (June 2025 release) and FY2027 budget (April 2026 hearing) more carefully in source citations.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively strong article that accurately covers an important climate story. The CO2 measurement data and quotes are all verified. The NOAA defunding threat section conflates two budget cycles in its Eos citation, which is recoverable with a correction. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
